coredata.dc:title: ATP-activated Fe(Ⅲ)/carbon dots assemblies for in situ generating nanozyme and simultaneously monitoring tumor cell ferroptosis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jian
Wang, Jian
Liang, Ke
Xue, Xiaokuang
Chen, Tiejin
Gao, Zekun
Ren, Haohui
Gao, Lizeng
Ge, Jiechao
coredata.dc:description: The integration of nanomedicine and nanocatalysis has currently resulted in the emergence of synthesized nanozymes with disruptive potential in alternative cancer therapy. However, the in situ synthesis of nanozymes using an intracellular components remains a formidable challenge. In this study, the inactivated Fe(Ⅲ)/carbon dots nanoassemblies (Fe/CDs-A) were prepared through Fe3+-induced assembly of red emissive CDs. In tumor cells overexpressing adenosine triphosphate (ATP), Fe/CDs-A can be activated and disassembled, causing the specific release of CDs and simultaneous formation of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes in situ due to the enhanced coordination between intracellular ATP and Fe3+. Intriguingly, Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes were found to possess peroxidase (POD)-like activity, efficiently generating highly toxic •OH in the presence of acidic H2O2 in tumor cells, thereby inducing tumor cells ferroptosis. Simultaneously, the released CDs initially penetrate the lysosomes of tumor cells and ultimately accumulate in the nuclei of dead/dying cells, achieving real-time monitoring cell death process through their recovered red emission. Overall, for the first time, the well-designed Fe/CDs-A demonstrates its capability as ATP-triggered inducers for in situ generation of nanozymes and specific release of CDs. This study offers a novel pathway for in situ generation of nanozymes for precise cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanozyme
ATP-activated
In situ generation
Ferroptosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and Characterization of CDs and Fe/CDs-A
ATP stimulus-responsive properties of Fe/CDs-A in solution
ATP stimulus-responsive fluorescence imaging and ROS generation in vitro
In vitro Cytotoxicity, ferroptosis mechanism and monitoring of cell death
In vivo imaging and anticancer Assessment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 52272052 and 22121003), the project of the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2022YFA1207600). All animal experiments in this work were performed in accordance with the guidelines of the Animal Ethics Committee of the Institute of Process Engineering (approval ID: IPEAECA2023065). The scheme 1b was partly generated using Servier Medical Art, provided by Servier, licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 unported license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of CDs and Fe/CDs-A. a) TEM image of CDs. Scale bar: 100 nm. b) UV–vis absorbance of CDs aqueous solution. c) The fluorescence of CDs aqueous solution under different excitation wavelength from 460 nm to 580 nm. d) FTIR spectrum of CDs. e) XPS full spectrum of CDs. f) High-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of CDs. g) TEM image of Fe/CDs-A. Scale bar: 200 nm. h) The fluorescence intensity comparison of CDs and Fe/CDs-A. i) XPS full spectrum of Fe/CDs-A. j) High-resolution Fe2p XPS spectrum and fitting results of Fe/CDs-A. k) DLS results of CDs and Fe/CDs-A. l) Zeta potential comparison of CDs and Fe/CDs-A in aqueous solution.

The ATP-responsive properties of Fe/CDs-A. a) Fluorescence intensity change of Fe/CDs-A under ATP stimulation. ATP stimulates peroxidase-like activity of Fe/CDs-A. b) With/without ATP stimulation TMB oxidation coloration experiment photographs. Padded pink color indicates the presence of reagent. c) The absorption of oxidized TMB (652 nm) treated with different conditions. (All groups were tested in PBS buffer with pH value of 4.5.) d) The fluorescence spectra of NaTA oxidized by •OH under different conditions. e) ATP stimulation ESR spectra of DMPO-OH with different treatments at pH 4.5. f) TEM image of morphology of Fe/CDs-A after the addition of ATP. Scale bar: 200 nm.

Characterizations and peroxidase-like activities of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes. a) The TEM image of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes. Scale bar: 1 μm. b) The FTIR spectra of ATP molecular and Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes. c) High-resolution Fe2p XPS spectrum and fitting results of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes. d) Absorbance values (652 nm) of the TMB oxided by Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes with POD-like activity at various acidic environment (pH 7.2 6.5 5.5 and 4.5). e) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB treated with Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes and H2O2 for different time. f) The fluorescence spectra of NaTA oxidized by •OH generated by Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes POD-like activity at acidic environment of pH 4.5. g) Michaelis-Menten kinetic analysis and h) Lineweaver-Burk fitting line plotting of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes with H2O2 as substrate. i) The illustration of fluorescence recovery phenomenon and generated POD-like activity of Fe/CDs-A in response to ATP.

a) Confocal laser scanning microscope (clsm) images of 4T1 cells incubated with Fe/CDs-A for different timepoints including 1, 3, 6, 12, and 24 h. Scale bar: 25 μm. b) CLSM images of Fe/CDs-A incubated HeLa cells for 6 h co-stained with Lyso-Tracker green and Hoechst 33258. Scale bar: 25 μm. The right inset: fluorescence intensity tendency of regions of interest of Lyso-tracker and Fe/CDs-A. c) CLSM images of Fe/CDs-A incubated HeLa cells for 6 h co-stained with Mito-Tracker green and Hoechst 33258. Scale bar: 25 μm. The right inset: fluorescence intensity tendency of regions of interest of Mito-tracker and Fe/CDs-A. CSLM images of Fe/CDs-A-incubated cells co-stained with d) DCFH-DA and e) HPF probes as ROS and •OH indicators, respectively. Scale bars: 25 μm. Blue channel: excitated at 405 nm; Green channel: excitated at 488 nm; Red channel: excitated at 561 nm.

Cell viabilities of HaCaT cells (human normal cells) and 4T1 cells (tumor cells) treated with different concentration of a) CDs and b) Fe/CDs-A for 24 h, respectively. c) Cell viabilities of 4T1 cells treated with 160 μg/mL of Fe/CDs-A for different time. d) Live/dead cell-stained (AM-PI) images. Scale bar: 200 μm. e) Western blot analysis of the GPX4 expression in 4T1 cells under different treatments. Ⅰ: Control, Ⅱ: CDs, Ⅲ: 80 μg/mL of Fe/CDs-A, Ⅳ: 160 μg/mL of Fe/CDs-A. f) Cells lipid peroxidation detection using Liperfluo probe. Scale bar: 25 μm. g) Mitochondrial membrane potential evaluation with JC-1 probe. Scale bar: 25 μm. h) Bio-TEM images of 4T1 cells with/without Fe/CDs-A treatment. Scale bars: 500 nm. Red arrows indicated mitochondrias. i) CLSM images of dead cells induced by Fe/CDs-A contained with Hoechst. Scale bar:25 μm. j) Illustration of the process of intracellular ATP activated Fe/CDs-A, inducing tumor cells ferroptosis and monitoring the cells death status.

a) Biodistribution of fluorescence in main organs (hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, and kidneys) and tumors of mice at various timepoints (1,3,8,12 and 24 h) after i.v. injection of Fe/CDs-A. b) Schematic illustration of the schedule of in vivo treatment. c) The relative growth curves over time of 4T1 tumor in mice treated with: I (PBS), II (CDs), and III (Fe/CDs-A). d) The relative tumor weights in various groups treated for 14 days. e) The photograph of 4T1 xenografted tumor in mice after 14 days treatment. Scale bar: 2 cm. f) H&E-stained tumor slices of different treatment group. Scale bar: 100 μm. g) Body weight changes of 4T1-tumor-bearing mice after various treatments in 14 days.

Schematic illustration of (a) the preparation of ATP stimulus-responsive Fe/CDs-A and (b) the process of in situ generating peroxidase nanozyme simultaneously monitoring tumor ferroptosis under ATP stimulation.

head.ce:title: ATP-activated Fe(Ⅲ)/carbon dots assemblies for in situ generating nanozyme and simultaneously monitoring tumor cell ferroptosis

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials
Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The inactive Fe(Ⅲ)/carbon dots assemblies act as ATP-activated inducers for in situ generation of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes with peroxidase-like activity and specific release of CDs. Fe3+-ATP nanozyme efficiently generate highly toxic ·OH in tumor microenvironment, inducing tumor cells ferroptosis. Simultaneously, the released CDs initially penetrate into the lysosomes of tumor cells and ultimately accumulate in the nuclei of death/dying cells, thereby enabling real-time monitoring of cell death process.

The integration of nanomedicine and nanocatalysis has currently resulted in the emergence of synthesized nanozymes with disruptive potential in alternative cancer therapy. However, the in situ synthesis of nanozymes using an intracellular components remains a formidable challenge. In this study, the inactivated Fe(Ⅲ)/carbon dots nanoassemblies (Fe/CDs-A) were prepared through Fe3+-induced assembly of red emissive CDs. In tumor cells overexpressing adenosine triphosphate (ATP), Fe/CDs-A can be activated and disassembled, causing the specific release of CDs and simultaneous formation of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes in situ due to the enhanced coordination between intracellular ATP and Fe3+. Intriguingly, Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes were found to possess peroxidase (POD)-like activity, efficiently generating highly toxic •OH in the presence of acidic H2O2 in tumor cells, thereby inducing tumor cells ferroptosis. Simultaneously, the released CDs initially penetrate the lysosomes of tumor cells and ultimately accumulate in the nuclei of dead/dying cells, achieving real-time monitoring cell death process through their recovered red emission. Overall, for the first time, the well-designed Fe/CDs-A demonstrates its capability as ATP-triggered inducers for in situ generation of nanozymes and specific release of CDs. This study offers a novel pathway for in situ generation of nanozymes for precise cancer therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data involved with this study are avaliable from the authors when reaonably requested.
Received: ((will be filled in by the editorial staff))
Revised: ((will be filled in by the editorial staff))Published online: ((will be filled in by the editorial staff))

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of peroxidase (POD)-mimicking activity exhibited by Fe3O4 nanoparticles in 2007 [1], nanozymes have emerged as promising alternatives to natural enzymes due to their inherent advantages such as adjustable catalytic capabilities, cost-effectiveness, robustness in challenging environments, and suitability for large-scale production [2–4]. Recently, the integration of nanomedicine and nanocatalysis has led to the emergence of smart nanozymes with disruptive potential in alternative cancer therapy [5]. These nanozymes not only harness the tumor microenvironment (TME) for in situ generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide anion (O2 •-) to achieve targeted therapeutic effects with minimal side effects, but also possess the ability to remodel the TME for effective eradication of tumor cells [6–8]. The current research has demonstrated that the tailored POD mimicking activity, which is specifically induced by the overproduction of H2O2 in the mildly acidic TME, holds great promise as an emerging cancer therapeutic modality [9,10]. This is attributed to its ability to catalyze endogenous H2O2 in tumors and generate highly toxic •OH, leading to selective tumor cell death. However, the catalytic therapy outcomes are significantly limited in the hypoxic TME characterized by micromolar levels of H2O2 [11] and high levels of glutathione (GSH, 1 ∼ 10 mM) [12,13]. To tackle these challenges, various strategies have been devised, including the design of nanozymes as TME oxidative stress amplifiers [14], the exploration of intelligent cascade nanozymes [15–17], the development of nanozymes possessing multicatalytic capabilities [18,19], and the design of single-atom-dispersed nanozymes exhibiting high catalytic activity [20–22]. Despite significant progress in the development of nanozymes for cancer therapy, the current strategy based on ex situ synthesis and delivery of active nanozymes to targeted sites, which may lack specificity and precision, thereby resulting in potential side effects.

The current trend in cancer therapy involves the intracellular in situ construction of nanomedicine via biochemical reactions including both covalent [23] and non-covalent interactions [24], which aims to enhance efficacy [25]. Compared to ex situ synthesis, intracellular in situ construction enables the direct fabrication of nanomedicine through a “bottom-up” approach by harnessing stimuli such as acidic pH, hypoxia, overexpressed H2O2, GSH and enzymes within the TME [26]. For instance, the evidence from numerous examples suggests that peptides or nucleic precursors can be specifically designed to form self-assembled supramolecules through enzymatic synthesis within cells, thereby inducing necrosis in tumor cells and inhibiting tumor proliferation and invasion [27,28]. The nanomedicine synthesized through intracellular in situ construction offers several advantages for cancer treatments, including prolonged drug delivery pharmacokinetics, reduced drug resistance, and specific target-tumor ability and others, leading to the highly effective cancer therapy [29]. Therefore, the intracellular in situ synthesis of nanozymes holds great promise for caner catalytic therapy and is urgently in need of development.

Very recently, an important breakthrough for intracellular in situ construction of POD nanozyme via endogenous GSH activation has been realized by Lin along with his collegues [30]. They initially constructed Cu-nanotransformer consisting of Cu2+/Phloretin co-loaded disulfide bond (−S-S-) bridged DMON (denoted as CDPh). In overexpressed GSH with in tumor cells, the degradation of CDPh subsequently occurred due to the breakage of disulfide bond, resulting in the formation of activated Cu (termed as ac-Cu-D). The obtained ac-Cu-D exhibited the activity of POD-like, therapy catalyzing endogenous H2O2 into toxic •OH, indicating a great potential in antitumor. In addition to GSH, adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is also overexpressed with in the TME due to the increased metabolism in the tumor cells [31,32]. Generally, the extracellular environment of tumors typically exhibits ATP levels in the range of hundreds of micromolar, while within the intracellular matrix, ATP concentrations are higher, ranging from 5 to 10 mM [33]. The significant disparity in ATP concentrations between the extracellular and intracellular environments have enabled ATP to be a smart target for cancer therapy, and various ATP-activated antitumor nanomedicine have been developed [34,35]. However, it is challenging to design an ATP-activated strategy for in situ synthesis of nanozymes to improve the efficacy of cancer therapy.

Herein, ATP-activated Fe(Ⅲ)/carbon dots assemblies were prepared for in situ generating nanozyme and simultaneously monitoring tumor cell ferroptosis. Firstly, citric acid-derived, red emissive CDs were selected as cargo carriers. Subsequently, Fe(Ⅲ)/carbon dots nanoassemblies (Fe/CDs-A) were prepared through the coordination effect between Fe3+ and the abundant carboxyl and hydroxy groups on the surface of CDs. The as-prepared Fe/CDs-A remained in an “off” state (inactive) in normal cells, however, in presence of tumor cells levels of ATP, it could be activated to the “on” state (active) due to the disassembly of Fe/CDs-A, resulting in the specific release of red emissive CDs and rapid formation of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes due to stronger coordination between Fe3+ and ATP (Scheme 1 a). The resulting Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes were further meticulously investigated and found to possess the characteristics of POD-like activity, enabling them to generate highly toxic •OH under acidic H2O2 conditions, thereby inducing ferroptosis in tumor cells. Simultaneously, the released red emissive CDs initially penetrate the lysosomes of tumor cells and ultimately accumulate in the nuclei of apoptotic cells, thereby enabling real-time monitoring of the entire process of cell death through their recovered red fluorescence (Scheme 1b). Both in vitro and in vivo studies demonstrated the in situ synthesis of POD-like nanozyme triggered by intracellular ATP was realized for precise induction of tumor cell ferroptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and Characterization of CDs and Fe/CDs-A
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized through microwave-assisted heating citric acid and formamide precursors referring to previous reports with modification [36]. In brief, 30 mmol of citric acid were dissolved in 30 mL of formamide and were microwave heated at 160 ℃ for 1 h. The obtained dark solution was collected and purified for further use. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image, as shown in Fig. 1 a, exhibited the typical spherical morphology of CDs with the diameter of approximately 3 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1b) displayed an absorption range from 300 nm to 800 nm with maximum absorption peak at approximately 520 nm. The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1c) revealed red fluorescence emission of CDs with slightly excitation-wavelength-dependent characteristic, and its maximum emission peak located at 620 nm. The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) in Fig. 1d was further conducted to investigate the surface structure of the CDs. NH stretching vibration peak at approximately 3389 cm−1, OH stretching vibration peak at 3159 cm−1, CO/CC bonds at 1632 cm−1 and 1603 cm−1, and CN/CO bonds at 1400 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 were clearly observed [37]. The full X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum (Fig. 1e) showed the three main elements of CDs: O1s (532 eV), N 1s (400 eV) and C1s (285 eV). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) revealed three different types of carbon element composition, including CC (284.7 eV), CN (286.0 eV), OCO (288.5 eV), which were corresponded to the results of FTIR spectrum. All results indicated that the water dispersible, red emissive CDs with abundant oxygen/nitrogen-containing groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups) on the surface were successfully prepared.

The abundant groups on the surface of CDs can effectively adsorb Fe3+ through coordination interactions. To systematically evaluate the coordination efficacy between CDs and Fe3+, a series of adsorption experiments involving CDs and various concentrations of Fe3+ were conducted. Furthermore, classical Langmuir and Freundlich isothermal adsorption models were employed to quantitatively analyze the chelating behavior of CDs towards Fe3+ and estimate its potential maximum adsorption capacity, as depicted in Fig. S1a. Experimental data indicated that as the initial concentration of Fe3+ increased, the chelating efficiency of CDs towards Fe3+ markedly enhanced, underscoring CDs’ proficient capacity for Fe3+ capture. In the fitting analysis of adsorption models, as shown in Fig. S1b and c, the Langmuir isotherm exhibited superior fitting performance compared to the Freundlich isotherm, with a linear fitting R2 value of 0.9974 (exceeding Freundlich’s 0.9659), illustrated with other parameters in Table S1. This result implies that the adsorption of Fe3+ on the CDs’ surface predominantly occurs in a monolayer fashion.[38] Based on the Langmuir model, the calculated maximum chelating capacity of CDs for Fe3+ was 55.1 mg/g, which positions it at a moderate level among published studies. These experimental findings solidly demonstrate CDs’ exceptional ability to chelate Fe3+.

By utilizing the coordination interactions between Fe3+ and functional groups on the surface of CDs, Fe/CDs-A was subsequently prepared. TEM analysis (Fig. 1g) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images in Fig. S2 revealed spherical nanoparticles with approximately of 25 nm could be observed. In addition, EDS mapping of the Fe/CDs-A was conducted. As shown in Fig. S3, the EDS mapping results visually confirm the successful synthesis and the uniform distribution of key elements especially the Fe element within the structure, suggesting the successful preparation of the Fe/CDs-A. Compared to the free CDs, Fig. 1h showed the fluorescence of Fe/CDs-A at 620 nm significantly decreased owing to the electron transfer process between Fe3+ and CDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching of CDs. FTIR spectroscopy was performed to investigate the changes of functional groups on the surfaces of CDs and Fe/CDs-A, respectively. Besides the shift of the stretching vibrations peak of CO at 1650 cm−1, a noticeable reduction in the intensity of the O-H stretching vibrations at 3190 cm−1 and the in-plane bending vibrations at 1400 cm−1 was observed (Fig. S4), indicating the involvement of hydroxyl and carboxy groups in coordination with Fe3+ ions. The appearance of the Fe peak in the full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1i) of Fe/CDs-A confirms the successful incorporation of Fe into the CDs. The high-resolution Fe2p XPS spectrum, presented in Fig. 1j, indicating the presence of Fe(Ⅲ), reveals that the Fe element maintain their oxidation state during the coordination process, emphasizing the stability of the formed Fe/CDs-A complexes and suggesting that the coordination occurs without altering the valence state of Fe. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) data, as shown in Fig. 1k, provides additional evidence for the structural transformation that occurs during the preparation of Fe/CDs-A. The significant increase in the hydrated ion radius from ∼10 nm for CDs to 30 nm for Fe/CDs-A implies the formation of larger aggregates or complexes upon Fe3+ coordination. This expansion could be attributed to the increased size of the Fe/CDs-A particles due to the attachment of Fe3+, which influence the hydration shell around the particles. Lastly, the zeta potential measurements offer insights into the surface charge modifications associated with the Fe/CDs-A formation. The shift from −40.8 mV for CDs to −19.4 mV for Fe/CDs-A indicates a partial neutralization of the CDs negative surface charge upon Fe3+ coordination. This change is likely due to the electrostatic interaction between the positively charged Fe3+ ions and the negatively charged functional groups on the CDs surface, resulting in a reduced overall negative charge. Collectively, these zeta potential measurements (Fig. 1l) confirm the successful incorporation of Fe3+ ions onto the CDs and their subsequent impact on the surface properties of Fe/CDs-A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ATP stimulus-responsive properties of Fe/CDs-A in solution
ce:section.ce:para: To comprehensively explore the ATP stimulus-responsive behavior of Fe/CDs-A in solution, we initially conducted a series of experiments aimed at elucidating the fluorescence variations of Fe/CDs-A upon the addition of ATP. As presented in Fig. 2 a, a pronounced and gradual augmentation in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs within Fe/CDs-A was discernible upon the introduction of increasing concentrations of ATP, spanning a range from 0 to 1.5 mM. This observation unequivocally demonstrates the ATP-induced fluorescence recovery phenomenon associated with the CDs, suggesting the ATP stimulus-responsive property of Fe/CDs-A in solution. As we know, GSH is also overexpressed in TME, and GSH have one thiol group and two carboxyl groups, therefore competitively coordinate with Fe3+ or react with Fe3+ to produce Fe2+ [39]. For this reason, the fluorescence change of Fe/CDs-A was recorded with the addition of GSH. As shown in Fig. S5a, no obvious fluorescence enhancement of Fe/CDs-A were observed with the addition of GSH in the ranges of 0 to 10 mM, indicating Fe/CDs-A was not responsive to GSH. Secondly, in addition to the observed fluorescence recovery, intriguing phenomena associated with Fe/CDs-A emerged upon the addition of ATP. As depicted in Fig. 2b and c, under acidic conditions (pH 4.5) with the presence of H2O2 (1.0 mM), the blue oxidized form of TMB and a corresponding absorbance peak at 652 nm were exclusively observed in the Fe/CDs-A solution upon the addition of ATP (1.0 mM). Notably, the absorption intensity at 652 nm for TMB treated with ATP-stimulated Fe/CDs-A was significantly enhanced. Conversely, no discernible change was observed in the absorption spectrum of Fe/CDs-A alone upon the addition of GSH across a concentration range of 0 to 10 mM, reinforcing the conclusion that Fe/CDs-A is not responsive to GSH (Fig. S5b). Furthermore, the o-phenylenediamine (OPD) colorimetric experiments (Fig. S5c) mirrored the findings observed with TMB, preliminarily suggesting the ATP-responsive peroxidase (POD)-like activity of Fe/CDs-A.

Thirdly, to further verify the POD-like activity of Fe/CDs-A with the ATP addition, sodium terephthalate (NaTA), the specific fluorescent probe for •OH was selected to detect the generation of •OH, as shown in Fig. 2d, in presence of acidic (pH 4.5) H2O2 (1.0 mM), the fluorescence oxidized NaTA at approximately 430 nm could be detected in Fe/CDs-A solution only with the addition of ATP (1.0 mM), suggesting generation of •OH. Furthermore, the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique was applied using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a trapping agent. As shown in Fig. 2e, under pH 4.5, the DMPO-OH signal was clearly detected upon the addition of ATP, confirming the capability of •OH generation for ATP-responsive Fe/CDs-A. The colorimetric experiments, fluorescence examination of NaTA as well as ESR technique all provide the evidence to confirm the ATP-responsive POD-like activity of Fe/CDs-A. As contrast, no •OH generation in free CDs, Fe/CDs-A without addition of ATP groups. Finally, TEM images were applied to investigate the morphology changes of Fe/CDs-A with the addition of ATP. As shown in Fig. 2f, the small nanoparticles with size of approximately 4.0 nm (blue arrows) and the larger nanoparticles (red arrows) with size of approximately 80 nm can be observed. The former could be considered as released CDs and the latter may be newly formed Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes due to the disassembly of ATP-responsive Fe/CDs-A.

Considering the Fe3+-ATP complexes capable of undergoing the Fenton reaction in the presence of acidic H2O2 has been previously reported [40]. We hypothesize that the POD-like activity of Fe/CDs-A assembly stimulated by ATP could be attributed to the formation of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes. To verify our hypothesis, a simplified model without CDs was established. Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes were directly synthesized through the self-assembly of Fe3+ and ATP, and the quasi-sphere morphology with the sizes of approximately 100 nm was observed using TEM, confirming the self-assembly behavior of Fe3+ and ATP, as presented in Fig. 3 a and S7a. Additional EDS mapping analysis on the Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes was performed to visually demonstrate the successful synthesis and elemental distribution within the structure. As exhibited in Fig. S6, the EDS mapping images further illustrate the spatial distribution of these elements within the Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes. The Fe mapping reveals a uniform and well-dispersed distribution of Fe3+ throughout the nanocomplexes, indicating that Fe element is homogeneously incorporated and not aggregated in specific regions. Similarly, the N and P mappings show a similar pattern of uniform distribution, confirming the integrity of the ATP structure and its intimate association with the Fe3+ ions. Moreover, by comparing the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3b) of ATP and Fe3+-ATP, the absorption peak of N-H at 3152 cm−1 in Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes was observed to exhibit broadening, while the stretching vibration peaks of CN (1712 cm−1) and P-O (1253 cm−1) shifted several wavenumbers. This implies a coordination interaction between ATP and Fe3+, potential coordination sites involving the amino group, the purine ring N atoms, and the phosphate group O atoms in ATP. The XPS spectrum in Fig. S7b confirmed the existence of Fe element in Fe3+-ATP, and high-resolution XPS analysis (Fig. 3c) revealed that the trivalent Fe could be clearly observed, indicating no valence changes occur during the process of coordination-driven self-assembly. Therefore, the formation of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes may originate from the stronger coordination between Fe3+ and ATP. Notably, as shown in Fig. S7c, the TMB colorimetric experiments induced by Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes solution indicated the obvious POD-like activity in acidic H2O2. Meanwhile, the absorbance of TMB at 652 nm (Fig. 3d) gradually increased in the pH ranging from 6.5 to 4.5, and the increasing absorbance of TMB along with the prolong time (Fig. 3e) could be observed. Furthermore, the increasing fluorescence of oxidized NaTA with increasing incubated time could be detected, as shown in Fig. 3f. Overall, all results further confirmed the POD-like activity of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes. Finally, to analyze the POD-like activity, the Michaelis-Menten kinetics characteristics of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes were determined using a microplate reader, referring to previous report [41]. By recording the absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm over time, as shown in Fig. 3g and h, the Michaelis constant (KM) for Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes was calculated to be 1.24 mM, while the maximum reaction rate (Vmax) was determined to be 1.10 × 10-7 M·s−1.

In brief, as indicated in Fig. 3i, in tumor cell ATP level, the fluorescence-quenched Fe/CDs-A can be activated, and the rapid generation of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes occurs due to the stronger coordination effect between Fe3+ and ATP, leading to the release of red emissive CDs. Remarkably, these Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes exhibit POD-like activity, enabling them to efficiently generate highly toxic hydroxyl radicals (•OH) in the presence of acidic H2O2. Consequently, this system holds promising potential for applications in cancer therapy and imaging, both in vitro and in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ATP stimulus-responsive fluorescence imaging and ROS generation in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the behaviors of Fe/CDs-A in tumor cells, fluorescence imaging experiments were initially conducted on HeLa cells co-incubated with 50 μg/mL of Fe/CDs-A at different time points. Under the 561 nm laser excitation in confocal microscopy, as shown in Fig. 4 a, the strong red fluorescence could be observed after 1 h post-incubation, indicating the recovery of red emissive CDs and the disassembly of Fe/CDs-A under the intracellular ATP stimulus. The red fluorescence of CDs exhibited a gradual increase within 1–12 h post-incubation, followed by reaching a plateau within 12–24 h post-incubation, as shown in Fig. S8. This observation indicates the enhanced uptake of Fe/CDs-A with prolonged co-incubation time, leading to an increased release of CDs. The detailed observation revealed a spotty distribution of released CDs within cells, indicating the potential localization of specific cellular organelles. Therefore, the distribution of released CDs in the HeLa cells was subsequently examined with sub-organelles-targeted fluorescent dyes. As depicted in Fig. 4b and c, the red fluorescence from the released CDs exhibited robust overlap with the signal from Lyso-Tracker-Green (a commercially available lysosomal tracker dye), yielding a high Pearson’s correlation coefficient of 0.81. Conversely, negligible colocalization was observed with Mito-Tracker-Green (a commercial mitochondrial tracker dye), registering a Pearson’s correlation of 0.09, and with Hoechst (a commercial nuclear tracker), yielding an even lower correlation of −0.03. Comparable findings were also replicated in 4T1 cells treated with Fe/CDs-A, as evidenced in Fig. S9, where the Pearson’s correlation coefficient for the red fluorescence signal of Fe/CD-A at 9 h post-incubation reached 0.82. These consistent results underscore the substantial accumulation of released CDs within cellular lysosomes. Subsequently, bio-TEM was conducted to observe the intracellular species to validate the generation of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes triggered by intracellular ATP. As depicted in Fig. S10, co-incubation with Fe/CDs-A resulted in the formation of distinguishable nanoscale aggregates (indicated by red arrows) that were distinct from subcellular organelles. Conversely, no similar nanostructures were observed in the absence of Fe/CDs-A co-incubation. This suggested the successful formation of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes within tumor cells. Finally, the ability of obtained Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes to generate ROS within HeLa cells was evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 4d, the intracellular ROS were detected using fluorescence probe DCFH-DA. Compare to the control group, the stronger green fluorescence could be observed in Fe/CDs-A treated group, indicating increased ROS generation in HeLa cells. Subsequently, 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (HPF) as the specific fluorescence probe was used to assess the production of •OH within HeLa cells (Fig. 4e). The observation of stronger green fluorescence in Fe/CDs-A group demonstrated the •OH generation capability of Fe3+-ATP in tumor cells. These results demonstrated that the in situ synthesis of POD-like nanozyme activated by intracellular ATP was achieved, evidenced by recovered fluorescence of CDs, bio-TEM images, and intracellular •OH generation.

Briefly, the inactive Fe/CDs-A can be taken up by tumor cells and subsequently disassembled upon intracellular ATP stimulation, leading to the release of red emissive CDs for lysosomal localizated cell imaging. Simultaneously, Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes with POD-like activity was in situ synthesized, efficiently generating toxic •OH in TME.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro Cytotoxicity, ferroptosis mechanism and monitoring of cell death
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the cytotoxic effect of Fe/CDs-A in vitro, the MTT assay was performed to evaluate cell viability. As shown in Fig. 5 a, free CDs did not display obvious cytotoxic effect towards HaCaT cells (human normal cell) and 4T1 cells (tumor cell) after 24 h incubation, suggesting the good biocompatibility of CDs in Fe/CDs-A. As shown in Fig. 5b, after 24 h incubation with Fe/CDs-A concentration in the range of 0 to 160 ug/mL in HaCaT cells, over 80 % cell viability could be achieved at 160 μg/mL of Fe/CDs-A, indicating the good biocompatibility of Fe/CDs-A towards normal cells. However, over 80 % 4T1 cells death could be observed at 160 ug/mL of Fe/CDs-A, exhibiting the significant cytotoxicity of Fe/CDs-A to tumor cells. Additionally, the impact of different incubation time at 160 μg/mL of Fe/CDs-A towards 4T1 cells viability was further investigated (Fig. 5c). The results revealed a gradual decrease in cell viability with increasing incubation time, indicating the sustained cytotoxic capability of Fe/CDs-A against tumor cells. The Calcein AM-PI staining experiments analysis presented in Fig. 5d, revealed only significant green fluorescence could be observed in 4T1 cells incubated with CDs. In contrast, in 4T1 cells incubated with Fe/CDs-A, a majority red fluorescence could be observed, indicating over 80 % of 4T1 cells death. The analysis of the live/dead cell staining experiments were consistent with the results of the cell viability assay, indicating the significant cytotoxic effect of Fe/CDs-A towards tumor cells.

To investigate the tumor cell death mechanism induced by Fe/CDs-A, firstly, western blot analysis was employed to analyze the protein expression in 4T1 cells after treatment with Fe/CDs-A (Fig. 5e). The results showed a significant inhibition of GPX4 protein expression (an important protein associated with ferroptosis) in Fe/CDs-A groups, whereas no obvious inhibition of GPX4 protein expression could be examined in control and free CDs groups. Secondly, Figs. 5f and S11 exhibited the strong green fluorescence originating from lipid peroxidation probe (LPO) in Fe/CDs-A group, indicating the high degree of lipid peroxidation. Thirdly, the 4T1 cells without Fe/CDs-A treatment showed strong red fluorescence from JC-1. However, the Fe/CDs-A treated 4T1 cells exhibited enhanced green fluorescence, suggesting that the mitochondria of the 4T1 cells were damaged (Fig. 5g). Finally, the cell morphology changes were investigated using bio-TEM images, as shown in Fig. 5h, compare to control group, Fe/CDs-A treated 4T1 cells exhibited swelling of mitochondria with vanish cristae. All aforementioned results adequately illustrated the characteristic of ferroptosis induced by Fe/CDs-A.

Particularly, during the cellular imaging experiments, we observed that after incubating tumor cells with Fe/CDs-A for 24 h, certain death cells nuclei exhibited significantly red fluorescence of CDs (Fig. S9,). Therefore, to ascertain the accumulation of CDs within the nucleus of dead cells, nuclear localization experiments were devised and conducted employing Hoechst as a nuclear-targeting probe. As illustrated in the Fig. S12, 4T1 cells incubated with Fe/CDs-A for different durations time was examined under CLSM. The results showed that with increasing incubation time, the proportion of blue fluorescence of Hoechst and red fluorescence of CDs overlaps gradually increased. By comparing with bright-field images, the regions of overlap precisely corresponded to the nuclei of dead or dying cells. Specifically, by quantifying the proportion of cells with nuclear CDs staining (as shown in Fig. S13a), we observe a gradual increase with prolonged incubation time of Fe/CDs-A. Concurrently, MTT assays in Fig. 5c demonstrate a decrease in cell survival rates with prolonged incubation time. This negative correlation between the fraction of cells with nuclear CDs staining and cell viability is further quantified in Fig. S13b, achieving the predictive power of CDs’ subcellular localization for assessing ferroptosis at the population level. Similar results could be observed in other tumor cells, such as EMT-6 cells (Fig. S14). Therefore, statistical analysis of CDs’ organelle localization patterns reflects the ferroptosis-inducing efficacy of the therapeutic system across the entire cell population. High-magnification CLSM images in Figs. 5i and S15 confirmed the released CDs indeed entered the nuclei of dead cells, thereby indicating that Fe/CDs-A not only exhibited a cytotoxic effect towards tumor cells, but also enabled nuclear imaging of dead/dying cells.

To elucidate the underlying mechanism of “reporting” cell ferroptosis, cellular imaging of CDs alone was conducted, as depicted in Fig. S16. The results indicated that, following 24 h of incubation, CDs localized specifically within the lysosomal compartments of cells with Pearson coefficient of 0.86, without any notable entry into the cell nuclei. This observation ruled out the intrinsic nuclear targeting capability of CDs themselves. In cells treated with Fe/CDs-A, it is plausible that as Fe3+-ATP POD-like nanozymes induce cell ferroptosis, the gradual accumulation of lipid peroxidation leads to increased membrane permeability of organelles. This enhancement facilitates the escape of CDs from lysosomes and their subsequent traversal through the nuclear membrane, enabling their entry into the nucleus to “report” the cells death.

Overall, as shown in Fig. 5j, during ATP-activated Fe/CDs-A induced anticancer, the as-prepared Fe/CDs-A initially were taken up by tumor cells, (step 1) and then Fe/CDs-A disassembled under intracellular ATP stimulus, leading to the specific release of red emissive CDs and the formation of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes with POD-like activity (step 2). The released red emissive CDs entered cell lysosomes, meanwhile, the formed POD-like nanozymes, Fe3+-ATP complexes, generated highly toxic •OH in TME condition (step 3). The generated •OH resulted in the cellular lipid peroxidation, the damage of mitochondrial membrane potential, the suppression of GPX4 expression, which induced tumor cells ferroptosis. In the meantime, the CDs escaped from the lysosome into the dead/dying cells nucleus, providing imaging and “reporting” the cells death status (step 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging and anticancer Assessment
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent cellular-level imaging and high tumor cell-specific killing effect of ATP-responsive Fe/CDs-A, further investigations were conducted on their imaging and anti-tumor effects in vivo. Firstly, the biocompatibility of Fe/CDs-A was evaluated, including hemolysis tests and examination of blood parameters and liver and kidney functions in mice after intravenous (i.v.) injection of Fe/CDs-A. No hemolysis was observed in the hemolysis test (Fig. S17a), indicating high blood compatibility of Fe/CDs-A. The results of liver and kidney function tests in Fig. S17b showed no significant differences compared to the control group, suggesting good biocompatibility of Fe/CDs-A. Subsequently, the pharmacokinetic evaluation of Fe/CDs-A was performed in normal mice through i.v. injection, and the blood half-life of Fe/CDs-A was estimated to be approximately 32 min using a dual exponential equation fitting, as illustrated in Fig. S17c.

A xenograft model of 4T1 tumors was established by injecting 4T1 cells into the right hind leg of female Balb/c nude mice (5 weeks old). When the tumor volume reached approximately 100 mm3, mice were i.v. injected with 4.0 mg/mL of Fe/CDs-A. Then, sacrificed them at different time points and collected major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) and tumors. The fluorescence changes in various organs and tumor sites were quantified using the IVIS spectral imaging system. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, compared to other organs, the tumor site exhibited a more pronounced fluorescence signal originating from the released red emissive CDs at different time points. Notably, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed in the tumor at 3 h post-injection of Fe/CDs-A, and the detectable fluorescence signal could be observed even at 24 h post-injection.These findings indicate the efficient accumulation of the Fe/CDs-A and successful disassembly of them at the tumor site. Additionally, the obvious fluorescence signals were also detected in the kidneys of mouse due to the excretion of the small size of CDs through the metabolic pathways of the kidneys. Whereas, no detectable fluorescence signals can be obtained in other organs such as heart, liver, spleen, and lung, further confirming the TME-triggered disassembly of Fe/CDs-A.

In in vivo anticancer experiments, the simplified time line was listed in Fig. 6b. 4T1 tumor-bearing mice with a tumor volume of approximately 100 mm3 were randomly divided into three groups for treatment: Group I (PBS), Group II (CDs), and Group III (Fe/CDs-A). The mice were i.v. injected with the respective treatments on the days of 0, 2, 4, and 6, and the tumor volume and the body weight changes were measured every other day. The tumor volume change curves in Fig. 6c revealed that the tumors in the PBS and CDs groups continued to grow rapidly, while approximately 80 % inhibition of tumor could be achieved in Fe/CDs-A group. After 14 days, the mice were sacrificed, and the tumors from each group were collected. As shown in Fig. 6d and 6e, the tumor volume and mass in the Fe/CDs-A group were significantly smaller than those in the PBS and CDs groups, respectively. Moreover, compared to Groups I and II, H&E staining of tumor sections in Fig. 6f showed obvious nuclear shrinkage and damage in tumor cells of Group III, further demonstrating the pronounced inhibitory effect of Fe/CDs-A towards tumor. During the treatment period, no obvious increasing body weight can be founded in all groups of mice (Fig. 6g). Furthermore, the H&E staining images of major organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidneys, all exhibited the similar morphology with normal tissue, as shown in Fig. S18. Key hematological data and liver and kidney function parameters in the treatment groups remained within the normal range, without significant abnormalities (Fig. S19). All results indicated the good biocompatibility of Fe/CDs-A.

In summary, the Fe/CDs-A was successfully conducted for intracellular ATP-activated in situ generating of Fe3+-ATP POD-like nanozyme, inducing tumor cell ferroptosis. Initially, under overexpressed ATP in TME, the inactivated Fe/CDs-A were triggered into “on” state, resulting in the rapid formation of Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes and the specific release of red emissive CDs. Secondly, Fe3+-ATP nanocomplexes illustrated POD-like activity in presence of acidic H2O2, facilitating them to efficiently generate highly toxic •OH in tumor cells, thereby inducing tumor cells ferroptosis. Simultaneously, the released CDs initially penetrate the lysosomes of tumor cells and ultimately accumulate in the nuclei of dead/dying cells, thus enabling real-time monitoring of the entire process of cell death through their recovered fluorescence. In vitro as well as in vivo investigation demonstrated that the intracellular ATP-triggered synthesis of Fe3+-ATP POD-like nanozyme was achieved, exhibiting significant inhibition toward tumor growth and good biocompatibility. This study proposed an ATP-activated strategy for in situ fabricating POD-like nanozyme inducing monitorable tumor ferroptosis, thereby representing a significant advancement in the field of nanozymes-based biomedicine for precise cancer therapy.

Jian Li: Visualization, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jian Wang: Visualization, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ke Liang: Validation, Software, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaokuang Xue: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tiejin Chen: Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zekun Gao: Validation, Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Haohui Ren: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Lizeng Gao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jiechao Ge: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 52272052 and 22121003), the project of the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2022YFA1207600). All animal experiments in this work were performed in accordance with the guidelines of the Animal Ethics Committee of the Institute of Process Engineering (approval ID: IPEAECA2023065). The scheme 1b was partly generated using Servier Medical Art, provided by Servier, licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 unported license.
